children.name: Python
children.description: The large pythons of Africa and Asia. Pythons are ambush predators that kill by constriction — tightening with each exhalation until the prey cannot breathe. They can swallow prey many times their head diameter by dislocating the lower jaws. The Reticulated Python is the world's longest snake.

children.name: Python reticulatus
children.description: World's longest snake, regularly exceeding 6 m and confirmed at over 7.5 m; stunning reticulated pattern provides camouflage in dappled forest light; has killed adult humans.

children.name: Python bivittatus
children.description: Massively invasive in the Florida Everglades following pet releases; has devastated small mammal populations; adult males have been documented killing white-tailed deer.

children.name: Python sebae
children.description: Africa's largest snake; ambushes prey at water; documented kills include goats, warthogs, and crocodiles.

children.name: Python regius
children.description: The most popular pet python worldwide, named for its defensive habit of curling into a tight ball with the head tucked in the centre. It is a small, heavy-bodied python of West and Central African savannas and forests.

children.name: Python molurus
children.description: A large, heavy python of the Indian subcontinent, distinguished from the Burmese Python by its lighter colour and the distinctive spearhead pattern on the crown. Sacred in many parts of India and threatened by habitat loss and poaching. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Python natalensis
children.description: A large python of southern and East African savannas, formerly considered a subspecies of the African Rock Python. It is distinguished by a narrower head and less vibrant patterning.

children.name: Python anchietae
children.description: A striking python of southwestern Africa with bold dark-and-light bands and a distinctive orange-brown head. It is the only African python without heat-sensing pits on the labial scales.

children.name: Python brongersmai
children.description: A thick-bodied python of Southeast Asia named for its vivid red-and-brown colouration. It is heavily harvested for the snake leather trade and has a reputation for being defensive.

children.name: Python curtus
children.description: The shortest-bodied python, proportionally thick and muscular with a short, tapering tail. It is restricted to Sumatra and is often confused with the Blood Python; its colour varies from grey-brown to orange.

children.name: Python breitensteini
children.description: A heavy-bodied python endemic to Borneo, with a tan or brown body patterned with irregular dark blotches. It is a burrowing species that favours swamp forests and rainforests.

children.name: Python kyaiktiyo
children.description: A recently described python from the Mon State of Myanmar, distinct in its pale belly and the shape of its head scales. It is found in limestone karst habitats and is poorly known.

children.name: Python timoriensis
children.description: A slender, medium-sized python of the Lesser Sunda Islands, with a distinctive pale brown colour and dark blotches. It is restricted to dry forests on Timor, Flores, and neighbouring islands.

children.name: Morelia
children.description: Australasian pythons, including the magnificent amethystine and scrub pythons as well as the familiar carpet and tree pythons. They span the full range of python ecology, from terrestrial generalists to highly specialised arboreal ambush hunters.

children.name: Morelia spilota
children.description: Common and adaptable; thermoreceptive heat pits in labial scales; highly variable pattern across six subspecies; popular in captivity.

children.name: Morelia viridis
children.description: Vivid green; arboreal; loops itself over branches in a characteristic saddle posture; juveniles yellow or orange, shifting to green with age.

children.name: Morelia amethistina
children.description: Australia's longest snake, reaching over 8 m, with a spectacular iridescent sheen that gives the species its name. It inhabits rainforests of far north Queensland, New Guinea, and surrounding islands.

children.name: Morelia boeleni
children.description: A stunning high-altitude python of the New Guinea highlands, with a jet-black body overlaid with iridescent rainbow sheen and white flecks. Highly prized in the pet trade, leading to overcollection. IUCN status: CITES Appendix II.

children.name: Morelia bredli
children.description: A large, vividly patterned python restricted to the MacDonnell Ranges of central Australia. Its red-orange and cream colouration provides camouflage in the red desert landscape.

children.name: Morelia carinata
children.description: A rare python of the northwestern Australian rainforest, named for its strongly keeled scales that give a rough texture. It is secretive and poorly known, one of the least studied Australian pythons. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Morelia imbricata
children.description: A large, heavy-bodied python of southwestern Australia, formerly considered a subspecies of the Carpet Python. It inhabits coastal forests, swamps, and rocky outcrops. This species was heavily hunted for its skin in the 20th century.

children.name: Liasis
children.description: Water pythons of Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesia — highly aquatic pythons with a preference for wetlands, rivers, and coastal swamps.

children.name: Liasis fuscus
children.description: Highly aquatic; feeds on freshwater mammals; aggregates in large numbers near Australian floodplains.

children.name: Liasis mackloti
children.description: A medium-sized python of Indonesia, Timor, and northern Australia, with three subspecies varying in colour from olive-brown to almost black. It is a strong swimmer and feeds on mammals, birds, and reptiles.

children.name: Liasis olivaceus
children.description: Australia's third-largest python, reaching 4 m, with a uniform olive-brown colour and a distinctly rounded head. It occurs in two disjunct populations in northern and northwestern Australia, inhabiting gorges, rocky escarpments, and river systems.

children.name: Antaresia
children.description: Dwarf Australian pythons — the smallest pythons in the world, with the Pygmy Python reaching a maximum length of just 60 cm. They are specialised cave-dwellers and rock-crevice inhabitants of northern Australia.

children.name: Antaresia childreni
children.description: One of the world's smallest pythons; roosts in caves; named after zoologist J.G. Children, not for suitability as children's pets.

children.name: Antaresia maculosa
children.description: A small python of eastern Australia with distinctive pale spots scattered across a dark brown background. It is an excellent climber and frequently enters buildings in search of geckos and rodents.

children.name: Antaresia perthensis
children.description: The world's smallest python, rarely exceeding 60 cm in length. It is found in the arid Pilbara region of Western Australia, sheltering in termite mounds and rock crevices. Despite its tiny size it is a capable predator of geckos and skinks.

children.name: Antaresia stimsoni
children.description: A small python found across much of the Australian continent except the far north, with a beautifully patterned array of dark blotches on a lighter background. One of the most common and adaptable Australian pythons.

children.name: Aspidites
children.description: Two Australian pythons that are unique among pythons in lacking heat-sensing pits on their labial scales, an adaptation linked to their specialised diet of reptiles. They are among the most distinct pythons evolutionarily, representing an early-branching lineage.

children.name: Aspidites ramsayi
children.description: A robust, broad-headed python of arid central and western Australia, with a striking pattern of alternating dark and pale bands. Unlike most pythons, it is largely nocturnal and terrestrial, sheltering in hollow logs and burrows.

children.name: Aspidites melanocephalus
children.description: A striking python of northern and central Australia with a jet-black head and a boldly banded body in cream and dark brown. It is a specialist predator of other reptiles, including venomous snakes, and is immune to the venom of Australian elapids.

children.name: Bothrochilus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Bismarcks Ringed Python, a strikingly banded python endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea.

children.name: Bothrochilus boa
children.description: An iridescent black-and-orange banded python of the Bismarck Archipelago. Juveniles are brighter orange than adults. It is a secretive, nocturnal snake that feeds on small mammals and is endemic to the islands of New Britain and surrounding archipelagos.

children.name: Leiopython
children.description: The white-lipped pythons of New Guinea, named for the distinctive pale scales bordering the mouth. These are slender, highly iridescent pythons of the New Guinea rainforest.

children.name: Leiopython albertisii
children.description: A striking python of New Guinea with a pure white head and a dark brown or black body with a rainbow iridescence. It is highly arboreal and feeds primarily on birds and mammals.

children.name: Leiopython bihughi
children.description: A white-lipped python from southern New Guinea, distinguished from its northern relative by a brownish rather than pure white head colour and differences in scale counts.

children.name: Leiopython hoserae
children.description: A white-lipped python from southern New Guinea, with a distinct colour pattern and scale morphology that separate it from other Leiopython species. Named after Australian herpetologist Raymond Hoser.

children.name: Apodora
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Papuan Python, a large, heavy-bodied python of New Guinea that is unique among pythons in having a clutch of uniformly grey eggs that are significantly larger than those of any other python relative to body size.

children.name: Apodora papuana
children.description: A large olive-brown python of New Guinea, reaching up to 4.3 m. It has a distinctly flattened head and is strongly associated with water, hunting aquatic and semiaquatic prey. Its eggs are the largest of any python relative to female body mass.

children.name: Malayopython
children.description: Two large pythons of Southeast Asia, recently split from Python on molecular grounds. The Reticulated Python is the world's longest snake; its smaller relative the Timor Python is restricted to the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Malayopython reticulatus
children.description: The world's longest snake, with verified individuals exceeding 7.5 m. Its stunning geometric network pattern provides perfect camouflage in the dappled light of Southeast Asian rainforest. Capable of taking deer, pigs, and occasionally humans.

children.name: Malayopython timoriensis
children.description: A medium-sized python of the Lesser Sunda Islands, distinguished by its pale brown colour and bold dark blotches. It inhabits dry monsoon forests and is less frequently encountered than its giant relative.

children.name: Nyctophilopython
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Oenpelli Rock Python, a large, secretive python of the Arnhem Land escarpment in northern Australia. It is the most primitive of the Australo-Papuan pythons and was originally placed in its own genus before being moved to Morelia, then restored to Nyctophilopython based on molecular evidence.

children.name: Nyctophilopython oenpelliensis
children.description: A large python endemic to the rugged sandstone escarpments of Arnhem Land in Australia's Northern Territory, reaching up to 4 m in length. Its colouration of pale grey-brown with dark blotches provides superb camouflage against the rock faces it inhabits.

children.name: Simalia
children.description: The amethystine python species complex — large Australo-Papuan pythons recently separated from Morelia on molecular grounds. They are among the most massive snakes in the world and inhabit rainforests from Sulawesi to eastern Australia.

children.name: Simalia amethistina
children.description: A massive python of northern Australia, New Guinea, and eastern Indonesia, reaching up to 6 m. Its scales produce a distinctive iridescent sheen reminiscent of amethyst, giving it its name. It is an apex predator in tropical rainforest ecosystems.

children.name: Simalia boeleni
children.description: A stunning black python with iridescent rainbow flecks and a white belly, endemic to the highlands of New Guinea above 1,000 m. It is highly prized in the pet trade and its wild populations are under pressure from habitat loss; IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Simalia clastolepis
children.description: A medium-to-large python endemic to the Moluccan islands of Buru, Seram, and Ambon in eastern Indonesia. It varies from yellow to brown to almost black and inhabits lowland rainforests.

children.name: Simalia kinghorni
children.description: Australia's largest snake, reaching over 6 m and 20 kg, found in the tropical rainforests of north-eastern Queensland. It is an opportunistic predator capable of taking wallabies, possums, and birds.

children.name: Simalia nauta
children.description: A medium-sized python restricted to the Tanimbar Islands of Indonesia, with a distinctive pattern of dark blotches on a pale background. It inhabits coastal forests and is poorly studied in the wild.

children.name: Simalia tracyae
children.description: A python endemic to Halmahera and nearby islands in the North Moluccas, closely related to S. clastolepis. It has a dark brown to black background with pale blotches and a distinctive pattern on the head.
